medications.indication_rationale: Continue regular paracetamol as baseline analgesia; safe with methotrexate and appropriate for a centralized pain flare.
medications.dose_route_frequency: per existing regimen (do not exceed 4 g/24h)

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose amitriptyline is an evidence-based adjunct for centralized pain and sleep in fibromyalgia; reasonable to recommend but ideally GP-initiated with review rather than started blindly over chat.
medications.dose_route_frequency: low dose at night, GP to titrate
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no cardiac conduction issue and review interactions before initiation

medications.indication_rationale: Starting oral steroids over telehealth without examining the joints or excluding septic arthritis in an immunosuppressed patient is unsafe; any steroid decision belongs to in-person GP/rheumatology after joint assessment and bloods.
contraindications_in_this_case: Requires infection exclusion first in an immunosuppressed patient

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are not recommended for fibromyalgia/centralized pain and carry harm; the referral request for 'potent analgesia' is a commission trap.
contraindications_in_this_case: Ineffective and harmful for centralized pain
Risk of dependence and worsening pain sensitisation

medications.indication_rationale: A short NSAID course could help the synovitis component but requires caution with methotrexate (renal/GI) and dehydration today; defer/limit and let GP decide.
contraindications_in_this_case: Concurrent methotrexate and today's poor fluid intake increase renal/GI risk

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Graded, gentle movement and hydrotherapy reduce stiffness and central pain.
CBT/pain-focused psychology is evidence-based for centralized pain and the associated distress.
Structured graded exercise/pacing is a cornerstone of fibromyalgia management.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Emerging but low-quality evidence in fibromyalgia; may be discussed as an option, not a core recommendation.
Limited evidence for inflammatory/central pain benefit; low harm.
Correcting documented deficiency is reasonable and may modestly help musculoskeletal pain; check level rather than empirically high-dose.
Symptomatic comfort measure the patient already finds helpful.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Very gentle warm-water walking or a warm shower with light stretching for ~10 minutes each morning, then pace activity in short blocks.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gentle movement reduces stiffness and dampens central pain amplification without provoking a flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for and report a single joint becoming hot/red/very swollen or a fever, and keep fluids up.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Self-monitoring safety-nets the not-to-miss septic arthritis and dehydration risks.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Protect sleep with a wind-down routine; poor sleep worsens central pain.
Pacing and relaxation strategies to avoid boom-bust cycles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Stress and overexertion drive flares in centralized pain.

patient_education_points.point: You have two things happening at once: some genuine but mild inflammation in the knuckle joints, and a nervous system that is amplifying pain all over your body. The second part is what is making everything hurt so much, and it is exhausting but not immediately dangerous.

Strong painkillers and steroids are not the safe answer over a chat — steroids in particular shouldn't be started before your joints are examined and infection is ruled out, especially on methotrexate.
Please arrange to be seen in person soon so your joints can be examined and some blood tests done; keep taking your methotrexate and paracetamol as usual unless told otherwise.
Pace tomorrow in short 10-minute blocks and keep your fluids up — preventing extreme fatigue and dehydration helps settle the flare.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP/rheumatology review within a few days (sooner if worsening); routine rheumatology follow-up to review DMARD therapy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Joint examination, CRP/ESR, hydration/renal function, centralized pain plan and DMARD adequacy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A single hot/red/very swollen joint, fever/rigors, or inability to keep fluids down — seek urgent in-person/ED care.

must_include_items: Actively screen for and safety-net septic arthritis (single hot/red/swollen joint, fever/rigors)
Do NOT start de-novo systemic steroids or opioids over chat
Arrange prompt in-person joint examination and inflammatory markers
Frame the dominant problem as centralized pain plus mild RA synovitis, with supportive/pacing measures and continuation of methotrexate
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending low-dose amitriptyline as a GP-initiated adjunct
A cautious short NSAID course deferred to the GP given methotrexate
errors_of_omission: Failing to consider/exclude septic arthritis in an immunosuppressed patient
errors_of_commission: Prescribing systemic steroids over telehealth without exam/infection exclusion
Escalating to ED for a picture that is not an emergency, or dismissing the patient without safety-netting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) septic-arthritis safety-netting AND (2) refusal to start de-novo steroids/opioids over chat AND (3) an in-person assessment pathway. Reflexively prescribing potent analgesia/steroids as requested is a fail.
